EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved from the gulf coast to offshore the Carolinas and up the east coast from the 18th to the 19th. This storm produced heavy snow across Garrett county Maryland, portions of northern West Virginia, and portions of Fayette and Westmorland counties in Pennsylvania. Total storm accumulations were from one to 2 feet in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia with over a foot in Garrett county Maryland. Storm totals were 6 to 12 inches elsewhere.

As warm air headed north ahead of low pressure moving into the Ohio Valley freezing rain quickly developed across much of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Untreated roadways quickly accumulated up to one tenth of an inch of ice making travel nearly impossible between 6 AM and 10 AM. Major interstates in and around Pittsburgh, including the Pennsylvania Turnpike were closed for more than 2 hours until roads could be treated and surface temperatures warmed above freezing. 911 centers across the area received thousands of call due to minor accidents. 2 more serious accidents resulted in 2 deaths, one in Beaver county, PA and one in Westmoreland county, PA.

A strong area of low pressure moved north across West Virginia to western Pennsylvania producing a heavy mix of precipitation. Snow accumulated 2 to 4 inches across northwest Pennsylvania on the night of the 27Th before changing to freezing rain the morning of the 28Th, then back to snow in the afternoon with another 1 to 2 inches. Ice accumulations in Pittsburgh and areas north were from a quarter to one half inch. For southeast Ohio, northern West Virgina, and Garrett county Maryland mixed precipitation changed to freezing rain during the evening of the 27Th with ice quickly accumulating to one half inch before changing to plain overnight. The rain changed back to snow during the afternoon of the 28Th with 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulating.

An area of low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley into the Ohio Valley on the 27th spreading precipitation across eastern West Virginia. Precipitation began as snow, but a change over to sleet, and eventually freezing rain occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Freezing rain finally changed to rain during the afternoon of the 28th. Rain ended during the early evening hours of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation averaged around one quarter of an inch across the county. Snow and sleet accumulation of one inch was reported in Lost River and Moorefield.

Low pressure developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast causing precipitation to develop over eastern West Virginia. There was enough cold air in place for precipitation to start off as a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain. Precipitation changed to all rain by late in the morning as warmer air was drawn into the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation around a trace was reported in Gerrardstown.

An area of low pressure passed through the Mid-Atlantic on the 10th into the early morning hours of the 11th. The track of the low was far enough north for warmer air to be drawn into the storm system above the surface. However, colder air remained trapped at the surface causing freezing rain to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice due to freezing rain was reported in Gerrardstown and Bunker Hill. Most of the icing occurred on elevated surfaces.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported near Circleville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the northern branch of the jet stream was able to phase with low pressure in the southern branch of the jet stream over the Southeast Coast on the night of the 18th. These two systems combined to develop a strong area of low pressure that slowly track up the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the 19th. The low was able to tap into moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean causing copious amounts of precipitation to develop. High pressure to the north kept plenty of cold air in place causing the precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 19 and 22 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient behind a cold front produced damaging winds across all of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, eastern Ohio, and Garrett county Maryland. Sustained winds were from 30 to 40 MPH with gusts over 60 MPH. The strongest reported wind gusts were 68 MPH in Franklin, PA and 66 MPH in Eighty Four, PA. Trees and power lines were reported down across the region, with more than 50,000 homes without power late afternoon on the 9th at the peak of the winds.

An area of low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley into the Ohio Valley on the 27th spreading precipitation across eastern West Virginia. Precipitation began as snow, but a change over to sleet, and eventually freezing rain occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Freezing rain finally changed to rain during the afternoon of the 28th. Rain ended during the early evening hours of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation from freezing rain averaged around one quarter of an inch across the county. Snow and sleet accumulation of 2 to 3 inches were estimated across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the northern branch of the jet stream was able to phase with low pressure in the southern branch of the jet stream over the Southeast Coast on the night of the 18th. These two systems combined to develop a strong area of low pressure that slowly track up the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the 19th. The low was able to tap into moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean causing copious amounts of precipitation to develop. High pressure to the north kept plenty of cold air in place causing the precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 20.0 inches at Shenandoah Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient behind a cold front produced damaging winds across all of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, eastern Ohio, and Garrett county Maryland. Sustained winds were from 30 to 40 MPH with gusts over 60 MPH. The strongest reported wind gusts were 68 MPH in Franklin, PA and 66 MPH in Eighty Four, PA. Trees and power lines were reported down across the region, with more than 50,000 homes without power late afternoon on the 9th at the peak of the winds.

An area of low pressure rapidly intensified over southern Canada. The pressure gradient between this strong low and high pressure to our south resulted in gusty winds across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 50 knots was measured at Upper Tract.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few late day thunderstorms developed in eastern Ohio ahead of a surface boundary.  That cluster moved southeast and pulsed to severe hail limits in portions of Tyler and western Doddridge Counties.  The freezing level was only around 7 thousand feet, while surface temperatures were only in the 60s.  While the thunderstorms were weakening, isolated wind damage occurred in southern Harrison County.

A potent cold front passed through eastern West Virginia on the evening of the 11th. A warm moist flow ahead of the front caused more instability than usual for this time of year. The combination of the cold front and instability caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down from thunderstorm winds in Maysville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak upper air disturbances helped trigger afternoon convection in a very moist environment, ahead of an approaching cold front.  The front was in northwest Ohio, Indiana and western Kentucky.  Clusters and short lines of thunderstorms formed in the late afternoon and again overnight.  The steering flow had increased to around 25 knots.  Storms pulsed briefly to severe limits with reports of both strong winds and large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed through the Mid-Atlantic on the 10th into the early morning hours of the 11th. The track of the low was far enough north for warmer air to be drawn into the storm system above the surface. However, colder air remained trapped at the surface causing freezing rain to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice due to freezing rain was reported in Petersburg. Ice accumulation was measured at thirteen hundredths of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast causing precipitation to develop over eastern West Virginia. There was enough cold air in place for precipitation to start off as a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain. Precipitation changed to all rain by late in the morning as warmer air was drawn into the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation around one hundredth of an inch was reported across the central and eastern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy rain producing thunderstorm prompted flash flooding in the city of Princeton. Several roads were closed due to the flooding, and the high water inundated a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow between an upper-level low over the Great Lakes and high pressure over the western Atlantic ushered in plenty of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico. The moisture combined with strong surface heating to produce an unstable atmosphere. Showers and thunderstorms developed due to the unstable atmosphere and an isolated thunderstorm did produce damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust was estimated around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure intensified off the New England coast on the 12th. A strong pressure gradient between this low and high pressure over the Ohio Valley resulted in high winds across eastern West Virginia on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down throughout the county. There were six brush fires due to downed power lines throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure intensified off the New England coast on the 12th. A strong pressure gradient between this low and high pressure over the Ohio Valley resulted in high winds across eastern West Virginia on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 62 knots were recorded throughout the county. The wind gust of 62 knots occurred at KMRB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest flow behind a cold front produced bands of heavy snow over the higher elevations of northern West Virginia. 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across portions of Preston and Tucker counties in northern West Virginia as well as Garrett county Maryland.

Cold northwest flow behind a cold front produced bands of heavy snow over the higher elevations of northern West Virginia. 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across portions of Preston and Tucker counties in northern West Virginia as well as Garrett county Maryland.

A weak area of low pressure passed through the Mid-Atlantic bringing a period of snow across the Potomac Highlands in eastern West Virginia. The Snow began late in the evening during the 12th and lasted into the morning hours of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of 3.0 inches of snow was received from Circleville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper disturbance passing across the southern Appalachians along with a strong northwest wind, brought an episode of northwest flow accumulating snow showers to Southeast West Virginia. This occurred from the late on the 3rd, until early on the 5th of February. The higher elevations above 2500 feet received four to six and a half inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across elevations above 2500 feet, in Western Greenbrier county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper disturbance passing across the southern Appalachians along with a strong northwest wind, brought an episode of northwest flow accumulating snow showers to Southeast West Virginia. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across elevations above 2500 feet, mainly in Western Mercer county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front passed through eastern West Virginia on the evening of the 11th. A warm moist flow ahead of the front caused more instability than usual for this time of year. The combination of the cold front and instability caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down from thunderstorm winds in Moorefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through West Virginia during the early evening of February 11th. A tight gradient existed between the low pressure over the northeast, and high pressure in the lower Mississippi Valley. This brought a period of high winds to southeast West Virginia through the morning of February 12th, which downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew down trees between Indian Mills and Forest Hill.

A deep upper trough and low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley pushed warm and humid air northward ahead of a cold front moving east with the surface low. Severe thunderstorms were widespread across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Significant flash flooding occurred over eastern Allegheny and western Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania with estimated damage of 18 million dollars to public infrastructure and private buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up through the Great Lakes. Plenty of moisture from the Atlantic Ocean and Gulf of Mexico was drawn into this system to produce widespread precipitation. There was enough cold air at the start of the storm for mixed precipitation, but warmer air did eventually work its way into the storm changing all mixed precipitation to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow fell in Gerrardstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong south and southeast flow existed across the mountainous counties during the morning and early afternoon hours on Christmas.  Peak gusts were near 60 mph, but most gusts were in the 45 to 55 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down a few miles northwest of Mullens.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations were estimated around one quarter of an inch. Snow accumulations of 2 to 3 inches were also reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds behind a cold front combined with the mountains to trigger snow across locations along and west of the Allegheny front. Snowfall amounts averaged between 4 and 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 and 6 inches were reported in Mount Storm and Bayard respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds behind a cold front combined with the mountains to trigger snow across locations along and west of the Allegheny front. Snowfall amounts averaged between 4 and 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of four inches of snow was reported in Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the northern branch of the jet stream was able to phase with low pressure in the southern branch of the jet stream over the Southeast Coast on the night of the 18th. These two systems combined to develop a strong area of low pressure that slowly track up the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the 19th. The low was able to tap into moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean causing copious amounts of precipitation to develop. High pressure to the north kept plenty of cold air in place causing the precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 26.0 inches at Circleville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the northern branch of the jet stream was able to phase with low pressure in the southern branch of the jet stream over the Southeast Coast on the night of the 18th. The low was able to tap into moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean causing copious amounts of precipitation to develop. High pressure to the north kept plenty of cold air in place causing the precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 15 and 21 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The mountain and the southern coal field counties were hit hard by heavy wet snow associated with a storm that moved out of the eastern Gulf of Mexico and across southern Georgia late Friday, the 18th.  The storm then moved off the North Carolina coast early on the 19th.  ||Heavy wet snow began over the southern mountains around Beckley after 1400E on the 18th. Meanwhile, the precipitation started as rain in the southwestern lowlands.  The storm even started as rain, as far north as in the Tygart River Valley around Elkins about 1730E.  In the Clarksburg vicinity the storm began mainly as snow around 2100E on the 18th. ||In about a 5 hour time span, 9 inches of snow had accumulated in Beckley by 1900E. The rain switched over to snow around 2100E for the lowlands, including Huntington and Charleston. By 2300E on the 18th, 14 inches of snow was reported in Oak Hill. Roads over the higher terrain quickly became hazardous and many were impassible.  Tractor trailer trucks jackknifed on the West Virginia Turnpike, resulting in a complete shutdown.  Unfortunately, 800 to 900 vehicles became trapped along a 3 mile stretch.  This occurred in the narrow mountain valleys between Chelyan and Beckley.  The vehicles were unable to turn around.  Many of these travelers had to wait 15 to 20 hours before emergency responders and highway crews could reopen the road.     ||The snow continued on Saturday with lighter snow rates.  The snow diminished during the late afternoon and evening of the 19th.  ||The western lowlands saw snow totals of mainly 5 to 10 inches, but the heavy snow failed to reach as far west as Mason or Wood Counties.  However, snow accumulations of 10 to 13 inches were common around Clarksburg, Grafton and Philippi.  Over Mingo, McDowell, and Wyoming Counties the snow accumulation was highly elevation dependent with totals from 6 inches along some river valleys to near 2 feet across the high terrain of eastern Wyoming and McDowell Counties.  Oceana, Mullens, Pineville, and War all reported a 14 inch accumulation.  Snow depths increased to around 2 feet in the Beckley and Oak Hill vicinity. These 2 feet snow depths continued northeast, to the mountains around Richwood and into Randolph and Pocahontas Counties.  Mount Nebo reported the deepest snow depth with 31 inches.  Pickens maximum snow depth was 26 inches. The maximum snow depth at Oak Hill was 23 inches, while Beckley had 22 inches, and Elkins reached 20 inches.|  |Tree limbs began to snap Friday evening over the coal fields of southern West Virginia above 1500 feet in elevation. The pop, cracks, crashes, and boom sounds were heard as numerous tree branches and whole trees fell to the ground.  The wet snow on the power lines even caused the poles to snap.||The worst impact of the storm, was the fact that electricity was out for thousands of residents for several days, including Christmas.  By December 28th, some 3000 customers were still without electricity in Mingo and McDowell Counties.  The American Red Cross had 16 shelters open.  However, only a few hundred people took advantage of those shelters. ||Governor Manchin declared a state of emergency.  The state provided several counties with water, generators, kerosene, oxygen cylinders, cots, and blankets.  National Guard troops assisted stranded motorists and helped with debris and snow removal.  The state asked the federal government to help pay for the recovery.

A cold northwest flow behind a cold front triggered snow showers across eastern West Virginia along and west of the Allegheny front on the 18th. An area of low pressure passed through on the 19th bringing another period of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bayard received 3.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air behind a strong storm system brought wind chill values to between 10 and 15 below zero to the higher elevations of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.

Arctic air behind a strong storm system brought wind chill values to between 10 and 15 below zero to the higher elevations of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.

Rapidly deepening low pressure tracked through the Tennessee and Ohio valleys December 2nd to 3rd and combined with a surface high over the western Atlantic to bring strong synoptic southeast winds to the area. The winds accelerated on some downwind slopes causing pockets of wind damage in some of the western and southwestern counties. Winds gusted to near 60 mph in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in various locations across the county. A southeast wind gust to 52 mph was measured at Bluefield Airport around 10 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The mountain and the southern coal field counties were hit hard by heavy wet snow associated with a storm that moved out of the eastern Gulf of Mexico and across southern Georgia late Friday, the 18th.  The storm then moved off the North Carolina coast early on the 19th.  ||Heavy wet snow began over the southern mountains around Beckley after 1400E on the 18th. Meanwhile, the precipitation started as rain in the southwestern lowlands.  The storm even started as rain, as far north as in the Tygart River Valley around Elkins about 1730E.  In the Clarksburg vicinity the storm began mainly as snow around 2100E on the 18th. ||In about a 5 hour time span, 9 inches of snow had accumulated in Beckley by 1900E. The rain switched over to snow around 2100E for the lowlands, including Huntington and Charleston. By 2300E on the 18th, 14 inches of snow was reported in Oak Hill. Roads over the higher terrain quickly became hazardous and many were impassible.  Tractor trailer trucks jackknifed on the West Virginia Turnpike, resulting in a complete shutdown.  Unfortunately, 800 to 900 vehicles became trapped along a 3 mile stretch.  This occurred in the narrow mountain valleys between Chelyan and Beckley.  The vehicles were unable to turn around.  Many of these travelers had to wait 15 to 20 hours before emergency responders and highway crews could reopen the road.     ||The snow continued on Saturday with lighter snow rates.  The snow diminished during the late afternoon and evening of the 19th.  ||The western lowlands saw snow totals of mainly 5 to 10 inches, but the heavy snow failed to reach as far west as Mason or Wood Counties.  However, snow accumulations of 10 to 13 inches were common around Clarksburg, Grafton and Philippi.  Over Mingo, McDowell, and Wyoming Counties the snow accumulation was highly elevation dependent with totals from 6 inches along some river valleys to near 2 feet across the high terrain of eastern Wyoming and McDowell Counties.  Oceana, Mullens, Pineville, and War all reported a 14 inch accumulation.  Snow depths increased to around 2 feet in the Beckley and Oak Hill vicinity. These 2 feet snow depths continued northeast, to the mountains around Richwood and into Randolph and Pocahontas Counties.  Mount Nebo reported the deepest snow depth with 31 inches.  Pickens maximum snow depth was 26 inches. The maximum snow depth at Oak Hill was 23 inches, while Beckley had 22 inches, and Elkins reached 20 inches.|  |Tree limbs began to snap Friday evening over the coal fields of southern West Virginia above 1500 feet in elevation. The pop, cracks, crashes, and boom sounds were heard as numerous tree branches and whole trees fell to the ground.  The wet snow on the power lines even caused the poles to snap.||The worst impact of the storm, was the fact that electricity was out for thousands of residents for several days, including Christmas.  By December 28th, some 3000 customers were still without electricity in Mingo and McDowell Counties.  The American Red Cross had 16 shelters open.  However, only a few hundred people took advantage of those shelters. ||Governor Manchin declared a state of emergency.  The state provided several counties with water, generators, kerosene, oxygen cylinders, cots, and blankets.  National Guard troops assisted stranded motorists and helped with debris and snow removal.  The state asked the federal government to help pay for the recovery.

Colder air poured into the mountainous counties, in the wake of a rain event.  The rain changed to snow showers during the afternoon of the 7th.  An upper level disturbance helped enhance the snow showers early on Thursday the 8th.  The snow showers lingered through that afternoon.  Snow accumulation of 3 to 6 inches were common. with local higher amounts.  Some representative snow accumulations included Marlinton 3 inches, Elkins 4 inches, Valley Head 5 inches, Richwood and Hacker Valley 6 inches.

Low pressure tracked up through the Great Lakes. Plenty of moisture from the Atlantic Ocean and Gulf of Mexico was drawn into this system to produce widespread precipitation. There was enough cold air at the start of the storm for mixed precipitation, but warmer air did eventually work its way into the storm changing all mixed precipitation to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation from freezing rain was reported around one quarter inch in Franklin and one half inch in Circleville.

These 2 feet snow depths continued northeast, to the mountains around Richwood and into Randolph and Pocahontas Counties.  Mount Nebo reported the deepest snow depth with 31 inches. Colder air poured into the mountainous counties, in the wake of a rain event.  The rain changed to snow showers during the afternoon of the 7th.  An upper level disturbance helped enhance the snow showers early on Thursday the 8th.  The snow showers lingered through that afternoon.  Snow accumulation of 3 to 6 inches were common. with local higher amounts.  Some representative snow accumulations included Marlinton 3 inches, Elkins 4 inches, Valley Head 5 inches, Richwood and Hacker Valley 6 inches.

Surface low pressure raced from the High Plains early on December 8th to the upper Great Lakes by early on the 9th while deepening rapidly and bringing a complex series of fronts across the area. Strong west winds of 20 to 40 mph occurred on the afternoon of the 9th with gusts over 60 mph estimated along some ridges and mountain peaks. Bluefield Airport ASOS measured a WSW wind gust over 56 mph twice between about 1:00-1:30 PM on the afternoon of the 9th and had sustained winds over 30 mph for several hours. The reported wind damage was to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were knocked down at the state fairgrounds in Lewisburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed through the Ohio Valley spreading precipitation across Virginia on the 6th and 7th. Warmer air was drawn into the storm system aloft, but surface temperatures remained below freezing resulting in freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter inch ice accumulation was reported in Falling Waters. Lighter ice accumulations were reported throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed through the Ohio Valley spreading precipitation across Virginia on the 6th and 7th. Warmer air was drawn into the storm system aloft, but surface temperatures remained below freezing resulting in freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of a quarter inch was reported in Slanesville on cars and sidewalks. Lighter ice accumulations were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure raced from the High Plains early on December 8th to the upper Great Lakes by early on the 9th while deepening rapidly and bringing a complex series of fronts across the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly weak area of low pressure developed along the Gulf coast and moved to the Carolina coast by early on December 5 bringing moderate snow to mainly higher elevations of southeast West Virginia. Snow amounts generally ranged from 3 to 8 inches with greater amounts at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall from 5 to 8 inches was observed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly weak area of low pressure developed along the Gulf coast and moved to the Carolina coast by early on December 5 bringing moderate snow to mainly higher elevations of southeast West Virginia. Snow amounts generally ranged from 3 to 8 inches with greater amounts at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 8 inches was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed through the Ohio Valley spreading precipitation across Virginia on the 6th and 7th. Warmer air was drawn into the storm system aloft, but surface temperatures remained below freezing resulting in freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 1/10 glaze of ice was reported to be covering all surfaces in Petersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly weak area of low pressure developed along the Gulf coast and moved to the Carolina coast by early on December 5 bringing moderate snow to mainly higher elevations of southeast West Virginia. Snow amounts generally ranged from 3 to 8 inches with greater amounts at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell, with the most in the western part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly weak area of low pressure developed along the Gulf coast and moved to the Carolina coast by early on December 5 bringing moderate snow to mainly higher elevations of southeast West Virginia. Snow amounts generally ranged from 3 to 8 inches with greater amounts at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county ranged from 4 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure raced from the High Plains early on December 8th to the upper Great Lakes by early on the 9th while deepening rapidly and bringing a complex series of fronts across the area. Strong west winds of 20 to 40 mph occurred on the afternoon of the 9th with gusts over 60 mph estimated along some ridges and mountain peaks. Bluefield Airport ASOS measured a WSW wind gust over 56 mph twice between about 1:00-1:30 PM on the afternoon of the 9th and had sustained winds over 30 mph for several hours. The reported wind damage was to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down in various locations across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley and emerged off the Mid-Atlantic coast on the 5th. This system spread precipitation across eastern West Virginia. There was enough cold air drawn in from the north for precipitation to fall in the form of snow across most areas. Most of the snow fell during the morning and afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall averaged between 2 and 4 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air poured into West Virginia on the 15th.  By dawn on the 16th the coldest temperatures were across the highest elevations.  Snowshoe dropped to 17 degrees below zero during the morning of the 16th.  For dawn on Saturday the 17th, a clear sky, calm winds, and a snow cover, allowed temperatures to drop even colder in the sheltered valleys. Glady was 25 degrees below zero.  This was the coldest temperature in the state since February 1996.  Some other minimum temperatures on the 17th included minus 18 at the Elkins airport, minus 17 at Bartow, minus 16 at Belington, and minus 14 at Valley Head.

After widespread rains, a strengthening low pressure system lifted northeast, through Michigan.  Its central barometric pressure dropped below 29 inches of mercury.||With cooling aloft and surface temperatures still in the 50s, surface winds increased during the late morning and continued through the afternoon.   Wind gusts of 45 to 60 mph were common.  A few stronger gusts were recorded.   At the Raleigh County Airport a gust to 62 mph was measured around 1233E.  At Yeager Airport near Charleston, a gust to 65 mph was measured around 1344E and another gust to 74 mph occurred at 1510E.  However, no damage was reported at the airport.  Along the Paint Creek around Standard, a mobile home was lifted off its foundation and slammed to the ground.  The home had been sitting on cinderblocks, elevated about 5 feet above the ground, to avoid the occasional high water. ||Elsewhere in the lowlands of West Virginia gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common.  A few trees fell onto houses.  One man in Putnam County was injured when a pine tree fell onto his golf cart.  He was standing next to the cart.  Scattered electric outages occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches fell in scattered locations throughout the county.   Electricity was lost in Elkins after tree branches fell on to power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved up the Ohio River Valley and overspread West Virginia during the predawn hours of Tuesday the 27th.  The snow quickly accumulated 2 to 6 inches by late morning.  The deeper snow accumulation was mainly concentrated across central counties from Mason and Jackson, on east toward Clay, Braxton, Nicholas, and Webster.     |   |By afternoon, the snow had decreased and transitioned to intermittent light freezing rain across southern counties. Further north, along the Route 50 corridor, the change to light freezing rain was delayed until around 1600E. The intensity of the freezing rain increased for the evening hours, with temperatures still in the upper 20s.  Ice accretions of around a quarter inch were widespread, with a half inch accumulation more common along the Ohio River counties.  The intensity of the freezing rain decreased after midnight, or early on the 28th, for the Ohio River counties.  Meanwhile, further east, temperatures rose above freezing, allowing just rain to fall.  The exception was Pocahontas County, where some freezing rain lingered. After the low pressure center lifted northeast through West Virginia during the daylight hours, the storm ended as a quick burst of snow in the afternoon. This added a coating of snow on top of the ice.||Numerous trees fell or sagged onto power lines. So the main problem was the loss of electricity.  Evergreen trees were especially vulnerable.  The trees were not only coated with ice, but the rain was absorbed by the snow that was already on the trees, adding more weight. A few homes and vehicles sustained damaged from the fallen trees.  A home in the Enslow Park section of Huntington was heavily damaged by a fallen tree. ||One power company alone reported over 45,000 customers without electricity.  The highest concentration was in the southwestern counties of the state, with Cabell and Wayne Counties being the hardest hit.  Some areas in that vicinity were without electricity for 3 to 4 days.  Motels that had electricity were quickly filled. Sheltered were open for those residents without auxilary heat.  The Salvation Army in Huntington housed about 40 people. One resident that stayed at home said, I am wearing two pairs of long johns and a blanket on may lap.  It's 50 degrees inside.  We are surviving. ||At a home supply store in Barboursville, people formed a line to wait on a delivery truck with another load of generators.  A power company spokesman said folks seemed to wait until after the storm hit and power was already out, before trying to find an alternative way to heat their homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure storm system was lifting northeast through central Kentucky during the late afternoon, then through Ohio overnight.   Southeast winds of 40 to 50 mph were felt over the higher terrain during the day.  A line of showers swept rapidly through West Virginia during the late evening of the 2nd into the early morning hours of the 3rd.  Gusts of 40 to 50 mph from the southwest were felt in a few areas with that line of showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Weston, siding on a house was ripped off and a porch roof was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved up the Ohio River Valley and overspread West Virginia during the predawn hours of Tuesday the 27th.  The snow quickly accumulated 2 to 6 inches by late morning.  The deeper snow accumulation was mainly concentrated across central counties from Mason and Jackson, on east toward Clay, Braxton, Nicholas, and Webster.     |   |By afternoon, the snow had decreased and transitioned to intermittent light freezing rain across southern counties. Further north, along the Route 50 corridor, the change to light freezing rain was delayed until around 1600E. The intensity of the freezing rain increased for the evening hours, with temperatures still in the upper 20s.  Ice accretions of around a quarter inch were widespread, with a half inch accumulation more common along the Ohio River counties.  The intensity of the freezing rain decreased after midnight, or early on the 28th, for the Ohio River counties.  Meanwhile, further east, temperatures rose above freezing, allowing just rain to fall.  The exception was Pocahontas County, where some freezing rain lingered. After the low pressure center lifted northeast through West Virginia during the daylight hours, the storm ended as a quick burst of snow in the afternoon. This added a coating of snow on top of the ice.||Numerous trees fell or sagged onto power lines. So the main problem was the loss of electricity.  Evergreen trees were especially vulnerable.  The trees were not only coated with ice, but the rain was absorbed by the snow that was already on the trees, adding more weight. A few homes and vehicles sustained damaged from the fallen trees.  A home in the Enslow Park section of Huntington was heavily damaged by a fallen tree. ||One power company alone reported over 45,000 customers without electricity.  The highest concentration was in the southwestern counties of the state, with Cabell and Wayne Counties being the hardest hit.  Some areas in that vicinity were without electricity for 3 to 4 days.  Motels that had electricity were quickly filled. Sheltered were open for those residents without auxilary heat.  The Salvation Army in Huntington housed about 40 people. One resident that stayed at home said, I am wearing two pairs of long johns and a blanket on may lap.  It's 50 degrees inside.  We are surviving. ||At a home supply store in Barboursville, people formed a line to wait on a delivery truck with another load of generators.  A power company spokesman said folks seemed to wait until after the storm hit and power was already out, before trying to find an alternative way to heat their homes.

The highest concentration was in the southwestern counties of the state, with Cabell and Wayne Counties being the hardest hit.  Some areas in that vicinity were without electricity for 3 to 4 days. We are surviving. ||At a home supply store in Barboursville, people formed a line to wait on a delivery truck with another load of generators.  A power company spokesman said folks seemed to wait until after the storm hit and power was already out, before trying to find an alternative way to heat their homes.

After widespread rains, a strengthening low pressure system lifted northeast, through Michigan.  Its central barometric pressure dropped below 29 inches of mercury.||With cooling aloft and surface temperatures still in the 50s, surface winds increased during the late morning and continued through the afternoon.   Wind gusts of 45 to 60 mph were common.  A few stronger gusts were recorded.   At the Raleigh County Airport a gust to 62 mph was measured around 1233E.  At Yeager Airport near Charleston, a gust to 65 mph was measured around 1344E and another gust to 74 mph occurred at 1510E.  However, no damage was reported at the airport.  Along the Paint Creek around Standard, a mobile home was lifted off its foundation and slammed to the ground.  The home had been sitting on cinderblocks, elevated about 5 feet above the ground, to avoid the occasional high water. ||Elsewhere in the lowlands of West Virginia gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common.  A few trees fell onto houses.  One man in Putnam County was injured when a pine tree fell onto his golf cart.  He was standing next to the cart.  Scattered electric outages occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches fell in scattered locations throughout the county.  Evergreen trees were most vulnerable.  Electricity was lost for brief periods.

An area of low pressure tracked through the Tennessee Valley into the Ohio Valley on the 27th spreading precipitation across eastern West Virginia. Precipitation began as snow, but a change over to sleet, and eventually freezing rain occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Freezing rain finally changed to rain during the afternoon of the 28th. Rain ended during the early evening hours of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of three tenths of an inch was reported in Ranson. Ice accumulation of one quarter of an inch was reported in Shepherdstown. Snow and sleet accumulation of 3.8 inches was also reported in Shepherdstown.

Precipitation began as snow, but a change over to sleet, and eventually freezing rain occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Freezing rain finally changed to rain during the afternoon of the 28th. Rain ended during the early evening hours of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation from freezing was estimated to be around a quarter of an inch across western Grant County. Snow and sleet accumulations around 2.0 inches were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through West Virginia early in the day, providing northwest winds that lead to an upslope snow event during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received from extreme eastern Mineral County of 4.5 of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports were received of snow accumulation ranging from 2 to 4.5 throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center tracked from Missouri to Michigan.  Meanwhile, its associated upper level trough pushed a cold front through West Virginia on either side of sunset.  A fast moving band of rain, along and immediately ahead of the front, featured a narrow line of embedded showers.  These convective showers helped mix down the winds that were located at 4 to 6 thousand feet above the ground.  Surface wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common. A gust to 73 mph was recorded at the Parkersburg airport. A gust to 85 mph was recorded on top of the exposed Bee Mountain in eastern Kanawha County.  ||Trees and large branches fell onto power lines and blocked roads.  The major utility company for north central West Virginia reported around 100,000 customers lost electricity before restoration began.  The highest concentration of these outages were in Harrison and Doddridge counties followed closely by Lewis, Upshur, Jackson, and Wood Counties.  In the southern counties, the major power company reported over 83,000 customers had lost electricity||Several buildings and homes sustained minor structural damages, while a few other buildings had significant or major damage.  At Twin Branch near Davy of McDowell County, winds caused the entire roof of a church gym to separate from the rest of the structure.  The roof then fell back down onto the cinderblock walls and nearby automobiles.  A 35 year old woman was killed while standing near the building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof of a barn was destroyed.

In the southern counties, the major power company reported over 83,000 customers had lost electricity||Several buildings and homes sustained minor structural damages, while a few other buildings had significant or major damage.  At Twin Branch near Davy of McDowell County, winds caused the entire roof of a church gym to separate from the rest of the structure.  The roof then fell back down onto the cinderblock walls and nearby automobiles.  A 35 year old woman was killed while standing near the building.

A strong low pressure center tracked from Missouri to Michigan.  Meanwhile, its associated upper level trough pushed a cold front through West Virginia on either side of sunset.  A fast moving band of rain, along and immediately ahead of the front, featured a narrow line of embedded showers.  These convective showers helped mix down the winds that were located at 4 to 6 thousand feet above the ground.  Surface wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common. A gust to 73 mph was recorded at the Parkersburg airport. A gust to 85 mph was recorded on top of the exposed Bee Mountain in eastern Kanawha County.  ||Trees and large branches fell onto power lines and blocked roads. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the northeast Gulf coast early on December 18th reaching a position near Alma, Georgia as a 997 mb low by Friday the 18th at 5 PM EST. The storm continued moving northeast and deepened to a 986 mb low near Cape Hatteras by 10 AM December 19th. Heavy snow began around midday on the 18th and snows rapidly accumulated to warning criteria levels by late afternoon or early evening in all of the West Virginia counties. Final snow totals ranged from 12 to 30+ inches. All forms of travel were rendered extremely difficult for several days due to this storm and numerous vehicle accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts ranged from 18 to 31 inches across the county, with the highest totals in the northwest near Quinwood. All forms of travel were extremely hazardous for the duration of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked from Texas through Iowa from December 24-25 bringing a very strong east to southeasterly flow across the area. The winds gusted to near 60 mph across southeast West Virginia and caused pockets of damage to trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree was brought down on some power lines in Bluefield and several other trees were also reported down. Bluefield ASOS measured a 51 mph wind gust at 2:52 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed through the Ohio Valley spreading precipitation across Virginia on the 6th and 7th. Warmer air was drawn into the storm system aloft, but surface temperatures remained below freezing resulting in freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of ice was reported in Moorefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure raced from the High Plains early on December 8th to the upper Great Lakes by early on the 9th while deepening rapidly and bringing a complex series of fronts across the area. Strong west winds of 20 to 40 mph occurred on the afternoon of the 9th with gusts over 60 mph estimated along some ridges and mountain peaks. Bluefield Airport ASOS measured a WSW wind gust over 56 mph twice between about 1:00-1:30 PM on the afternoon of the 9th and had sustained winds over 30 mph for several hours. The reported wind damage was to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed through the Ohio Valley spreading precipitation across Virginia on the 6th and 7th. Warmer air was drawn into the storm system aloft, but surface temperatures remained below freezing resulting in freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation of 1/10 was reported in Berkeley Springs on trees and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed during Saturday evening, the 21st.  A brief period of rain occurred with the front. However, a quick change to snow occurred following the frontal passage as temperatures quickly dropped.||During the late night and early morning, the main precipitation shifted into the baroclinic zone behind the front, resulting in 1 to 4 inches of snow by dawn on Sunday the 22nd.  A sharp mid level disturbance crossed during Sunday morning.  This enhanced the snow rates for a few hours.  Following the passage of that disturbance, the more typical upslope scenario developed.  Low level lapse rates were steep, courtesy of the cold pool of air aloft.  The snow became more cellular in nature for the afternoon hours.  Accumulations during the afternoon were limited to around an inch.  This was due to the showery nature of the snow and the increasing sun angle of late February.  ||The snow showers continued overnight, aided by another mid level disturbance during the predawn hours of Monday the 23rd.  The snow showers finally diminished to flurries by Monday afternoon.||Total snow accumulations were mostly 4 to 8 inches.  Locally higher amounts occurred in the favored upslope areas at high elevations. For example, the snow depth at Snowshoe went from around 4 inches prior to this event to 14 inches.  Pickens snow depth went from a trace to 10 inches.  On Point Mountain near Monterville and at Kumbrabow State Forest, the average snow depth reached around 20 inches. ||In contrast, some of the protected valleys, such as the Marlinton and Elkins vicinity, received less snow, about 2 to 4 inches in accumulation.             ||In the wake of this snow event, temperatures dropped below zero in many of the mountain valleys for dawn on the 24th.  Glady of Randolph County was 8 below zero for one of the coldest readings.

A strong upper air disturbance triggered a relatively small but heavy band of snow.  The snow moved east, out of northern Kentucky and into southern West Virginia, during the afternoon of the 3rd.  The heaviest snow remained south of Huntington and Charleston, affecting the southern coal fields.  This feature, tracking across southern West Virginia, delayed the start of the snow for the central mountain counties around Summersville to Elkins. ||By late that night, 4 to 7 inches of snow had accumulated over Mingo and Logan Counties.  For example, Lorado had 7 inches, Naugatuck had 6 inches, and Chapmanville measured 5 inches.  ||By the predawn hours of Wednesday the 4th, another upper air disturbance, low level moisture, and the colder air helped develop snow showers further north. As a result, the snow showers increased over the central mountain counties of West Virginia.  Meanwhile, fluffy snow showers lingered over the southern mountain counties.  ||By late Wednesday afternoon, 4 to 8 inches of snow was common across Fayette, Raleigh, Wyoming, and Mcdowell Counties.  Some representative snow accumulations were 8 inches at Cool Ridge, 6 inches at Cyclone and Lookout, 5 inches at Beckley, Oak Hill, and Iaeger. ||Across the central mountain counties, the fluffy snow showers lingered into the early morning hours of Thursday the 5th.  Total snow accumulations were widely variable across Webster, Nicholas, Pocahontas, and Randolph.  The higher windward terrain had over a foot of snow. This includes places like Point Mountain of Webster County, the high terrain around Pickens, Snowshoe, and across Cheat Mountain.  In Nicholas County the mountains around Richwood reported 8 inches.  Meanwhile, some river valley communities only measured 2 to 4 inches of accumulation.

Accumulations during the afternoon were limited to around an inch.  This was due to the showery nature of the snow and the increasing sun angle of late February.  ||The snow showers continued overnight, aided by another mid level disturbance during the predawn hours of Monday the 23rd.  The snow showers finally diminished to flurries by Monday afternoon.||Total snow accumulations were mostly 4 to 8 inches.  Locally higher amounts occurred in the favored upslope areas at high elevations. For example, the snow depth at Snowshoe went from around 4 inches prior to this event to 14 inches.  Pickens snow depth went from a trace to 10 inches.  On Point Mountain near Monterville and at Kumbrabow State Forest, the average snow depth reached around 20 inches. ||In contrast, some of the protected valleys, such as the Marlinton and Elkins vicinity, received less snow, about 2 to 4 inches in accumulation.             ||In the wake of this snow event, temperatures dropped below zero in many of the mountain valleys for dawn on the 24th.  Glady of Randolph County was 8 below zero for one of the coldest readings.

However, a quick change to snow occurred following the frontal passage as temperatures quickly dropped.||During the late night and early morning, the main precipitation shifted into the baroclinic zone behind the front, resulting in 1 to 4 inches of snow by dawn on Sunday the 22nd. A strong low pressure center tracked from Missouri to Michigan.  Meanwhile, its associated upper level trough pushed a cold front through West Virginia on either side of sunset.  A fast moving band of rain, along and immediately ahead of the front, featured a narrow line of embedded showers.  These convective showers helped mix down the winds that were located at 4 to 6 thousand feet above the ground.  Surface wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common. A gust to 73 mph was recorded at the Parkersburg airport. A gust to 85 mph was recorded on top of the exposed Bee Mountain in eastern Kanawha County.  ||Trees and large branches fell onto power lines and blocked roads.  The major utility company for north central West Virginia reported around 100,000 customers lost electricity before restoration began.  The highest concentration of these outages were in Harrison and Doddridge counties followed closely by Lewis, Upshur, Jackson, and Wood Counties.  In the southern counties, the major power company reported over 83,000 customers had lost electricity||Several buildings and homes sustained minor structural damages, while a few other buildings had significant or major damage.  At Twin Branch near Davy of McDowell County, winds caused the entire roof of a church gym to separate from the rest of the structure.  The roof then fell back down onto the cinderblock walls and nearby automobiles.  A 35 year old woman was killed while standing near the building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wooden steeple to a church collapsed onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper air disturbance triggered a relatively small but heavy band of snow.  The snow moved east, out of northern Kentucky and into southern West Virginia, during the afternoon of the 3rd.  The heaviest snow remained south of Huntington and Charleston, affecting the southern coal fields.  This feature, tracking across southern West Virginia, delayed the start of the snow for the central mountain counties around Summersville to Elkins. ||By late that night, 4 to 7 inches of snow had accumulated over Mingo and Logan Counties.  For example, Lorado had 7 inches, Naugatuck had 6 inches, and Chapmanville measured 5 inches.  ||By the predawn hours of Wednesday the 4th, another upper air disturbance, low level moisture, and the colder air helped develop snow showers further north. As a result, the snow showers increased over the central mountain counties of West Virginia.  Meanwhile, fluffy snow showers lingered over the southern mountain counties.  ||By late Wednesday afternoon, 4 to 8 inches of snow was common across Fayette, Raleigh, Wyoming, and Mcdowell Counties.  Some representative snow accumulations were 8 inches at Cool Ridge, 6 inches at Cyclone and Lookout, 5 inches at Beckley, Oak Hill, and Iaeger. ||Across the central mountain counties, the fluffy snow showers lingered into the early morning hours of Thursday the 5th.  Total snow accumulations were widely variable across Webster, Nicholas, Pocahontas, and Randolph.  The higher windward terrain had over a foot of snow. This includes places like Point Mountain of Webster County, the high terrain around Pickens, Snowshoe, and across Cheat Mountain.  In Nicholas County the mountains around Richwood reported 8 inches.  Meanwhile, some river valley communities only measured 2 to 4 inches of accumulation.

A powerful cold front moved across the Ohio Valley on the evening of the 11th as strong low pressure moved north across the Great Lakes. A convective line along the cold front produced widespread damage across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland with thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH and a peak wind gust of 92 MPH at Allegheny County Airport near Pittsburgh. High winds gusting to 60 MPH behind the front in a strong pressure gradient produced wind damage across the entire region into midday on the 12th. In all across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland over one half million homes and businesses were without power at some point during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure intensified off the New England coast on the 12th. A strong pressure gradient between this low and high pressure over the Ohio Valley resulted in high winds across eastern West Virginia on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 knots was measured at KCBE.

A fast moving band of rain, along and immediately ahead of the front, featured a narrow line of embedded showers.  These convective showers helped mix down the winds that were located at 4 to 6 thousand feet above the ground.  Surface wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common. A gust to 73 mph was recorded at the Parkersburg airport. A gust to 85 mph was recorded on top of the exposed Bee Mountain in eastern Kanawha County.  ||Trees and large branches fell onto power lines and blocked roads.  The major utility company for north central West Virginia reported around 100,000 customers lost electricity before restoration began.  The highest concentration of these outages were in Harrison and Doddridge counties followed closely by Lewis, Upshur, Jackson, and Wood Counties.  In the southern counties, the major power company reported over 83,000 customers had lost electricity||Several buildings and homes sustained minor structural damages, while a few other buildings had significant or major damage.  At Twin Branch near Davy of McDowell County, winds caused the entire roof of a church gym to separate from the rest of the structure.  The roof then fell back down onto the cinderblock walls and nearby automobiles.  A 35 year old woman was killed while standing near the building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system passed through the Mid-Atlantic bringing a period of snow and ice to eastern West Virginia. The wintry precipitation fell during the morning hours of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of ice was reported in Riverton. Snow accumulation averaged around one inch or less across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest flow behind a cold front triggered snow showers across eastern West Virginia for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 3.0 inches in Cherry Grove.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 6.0 inches in Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold moist air behind a cold front triggered upslope snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny front across eastern West Virginia. The snow began on the morning of the 22nd and lasted into the afternoon hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 10.0 inches in Circleville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold moist air behind a cold front triggered upslope snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny front across eastern West Virginia. The snow began on the morning of the 22nd and lasted into the afternoon hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 11.0 inches in Bayard. A report of 7.0 inches of snow was also received from Mount Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abandoned underground mine in Coalburg Mountain near East Bank had filled with water.  After a 4 day rainy period that saw 1.75 to 2 inches of rain fall, the mine blew out the side of the mountain during the predawn hours.  Water gushed out of the mine and cascaded down the mountainside.  Rocks and mud were picked up by the flowing current. Debris was deposited across roads and railroad tracks on the western end of the community.  A parked dump truck was shoved over an embankment.  A few homes were evacuated. Water and mud got into at least one garage and basement. Classes at East Bank Middle School were canceled.  After a few hours, the water continued to drain, but with decreasing quantity.  It took highway maintenance crews a week of work to reopen Route 61.

A northwest flow of cold air, combined with an upper disturbance, brought accumulating snows to the higher elevations of western Greenbrier County on February 22nd and 23rd. As much as 10 inches of snow fell across elevations above 2500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 10 inches of snow fell across western portions of Greenbrier County, at elevations above 2500 feet. Several locations saw 10 inches, including Mcross, Quinwood, and Russellville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through West Virginia during the early evening of February 11th. A tight gradient existed between the low pressure over the northeast, and high pressure in the lower Mississippi Valley. This brought a period of high winds to southeast West Virginia through the morning of February 12th, which downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew down trees around the Princeton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system moved up the Ohio River Valley and into southern Ohio.  Near and south of the low pressure center, a line of showers and thunderstorms formed as dew points rose into the lower 50s.  The precipitation raced east and northeast at 50 to 55 mph, through northeast Kentucky and into southwest West Virginia.||Around 1500 customers lost electric power in Logan County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was measured at the Raleigh County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper level low tracked east, into central Kentucky by late afternoon. However, the associated surface low pressure was gradually weakening. ||After some morning showers, the sky cleared and temperatures climbed toward 70 degrees across southern West Virginia. However, dew points did not increase dramatically, only reaching into the upper 40s by late afternoon. ||A band of convection formed ahead of the upper level system, where a few stronger cells formed on the northern side of the complex.  Before weakening, one cell produced large hail in Wayne County.  Rains of 1 to 1.5 were common.

A squall line along and ahead of a cold front moved across southeast West Virginia during the evening of February 11th. Some of the showers and storms along this line produced damaging winds which downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds blew the roof off a motel on Oakvale Road in Princeton, WV. The roof crashed into a nearby restaurant, where 12 people had to be evacuated through broken windows. Several vehicles were damaged, with only minor injuries reported. Damages with this incident are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of 7.5 inches of snow was received in Bayard. Mount Storm also reported 6.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving slowly across West Virginia produced widespread heavy rain and flooding across northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and western Pennsylvania. Rain amounts over a 24 to 36 hour period were from 2 to 3 inches on top of soils that were already saturated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads flooded across the county with bulk of high water along the West Fork River around Worthington. SR 19 flooded with some water in structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist environment yielded mostly heavy rain in the showers and thunderstorms on the 26th.  However, one storm pulsed briefly to a stronger level, and caused large hail to reach the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere triggered showers and thunderstorms. Locally heavy amounts of rain from thunderstorms in a short period of time lead to an isolated flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report came in to the Mineral County 911 center of a basement flooded with 4 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low level winds combined with a disturbance passing across the Central Appalachians to generate thunderstorms with damaging winds during the early morning of the 31st of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down in the town of Princeton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Camp Creek flooded in the Camp Creek State Park area, leading to evacuations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms approached western West Virginia from southern Ohio and northern Kentucky by early evening on the 30th. The region was in a northwest flow aloft.  Some of the thunderstorms strengthened, with high reflectivity cores observed on weather radar. This resulted in some large hail reaching the ground.  Damaging wind gusts also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

Thunderstorms fired across southeast Ohio during the heat and instability of the afternoon and moved into West Virginia.  The area was well south of an east to west front.  The storms became multicellular and formed short lines. Numerous outflow boundaries were produced.||Large hail was noted quickly, with some strong wind gusts developing as the episode unfolded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were stripped of their leaves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After over an inch of rain in the past 24 hours, another round of showers and thunderstorms moved through southern West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 11th.  Due to the wet ground and steep terrain, minor flooding occurred when another inch of rain fell in about an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gilbert Creek flooded sections of Route 52.  A mudslide blocked Route 49 in Thacker Hollow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days of wet weather, another round of showers fell from the predawn hours on the 7th into the mid morning.  Training of small heavier cells occurred across southern Lewis into Upshur Counties.  Rain amounts of a .5 to .75 in 3 hours were seen.  Total rain amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches occurred. With the new spring vegetation just  emerging, this was enough rain to cause small streams to overflow and block roads.  Route 19 in southern Lewis County was affected.

A wave of low pressure moving along a slow moving cold front produced scattered severe thunderstorms across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.

A large complex of showers and thunderstorms moved east, out of northern Kentucky, passing through southwestern West Virginia during the late morning and early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized downpours hit the city of Huntington during the late morning and into the early afternoon on the 10th.  Measured rain amounts for the 24 hour period were in the 2.4 to 2.5 inch range.  Most of that rain fell in about 2 hours. ||The downpours overwhelmed the city drainage system.  The railroad viaducts flooded, causing temporary road closures and detours. About a dozen vehicles stalled out in standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an unstable atmosphere to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of the third. An isolated thunderstorm did become severe, producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Inkerman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere lead to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms on the 17th across eastern West Virginia. A couple thunderstorms did become severe, producing damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were down o Patterson Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure moving along a slow moving cold front produced scattered severe thunderstorms across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.

A front was stretched out east to west near the southern border of West Virginia. A strong mid level disturbance lifted northeast and enhanced the rain after 0100E on Monday the 4th before lifting out around  noon.  The axis of the heaviest rain fell from southern Wayne County on northeast, through western Kanawaha County, southern Roane County, southern Calhoun County, then through Gilmer, Lewis, and Harrison Counties.  The rain totals in this maximum were mostly 2 to 2.9 inches in a 6 to 12 hour period.||Leading up to this episode, the ground was saturated from light but frequent rain. The spring foliage was just in its beginning stages, and not consuming a lot of soil moisture.  As a result, the rain quickly became runoff.||Nuiisance flooding was common, with water ponding on roads in poor drainage areas.  More serious stream flooding occurred along the West Fork of the Little Kanawha River in Calhoun County. Schools were canceled in a few counties. ||River flooding resulted along the Little Kanawha River downstream of Burnsville through Glenville to Grantsville.  The West Fork River also flooded as it flowed north through Harrison County.||In June, some of these counties were added to FEMA disaster 1838.  Around 3 dozen home owners applied for assistance.

A stalled boundary remained over West Virginia during the evening and overnight hours of the 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th. Showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of this boundary. With plenty of moisture throughout the atmosphere, thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain which lead to areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 220 near Stony Run Road was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with good upper dynamics, but with a subpar low level jet, yielded a few lines of thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms were widespread, but the severe reports were quite sporadic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms approached western West Virginia from southern Ohio and northern Kentucky by early evening on the 30th. The region was in a northwest flow aloft.  Some of the thunderstorms strengthened, with high reflectivity cores observed on weather radar. This resulted in some large hail reaching the ground.  Damaging wind gusts also occurred.

Thunderstorms fired across southeast Ohio during the heat and instability of the afternoon and moved into West Virginia.  The area was well south of an east to west front.  The storms became multicellular and formed short lines. Numerous outflow boundaries were produced.||Large hail was noted quickly, with some strong wind gusts developing as the episode unfolded.

A stalled boundary remained over West Virginia during the evening and overnight hours of the 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th. Showers and thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of this boundary. With plenty of moisture throughout the atmosphere, thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain which lead to areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 46 was closed due to flash flooding in the Fountain area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small stream flooding was followed quickly by river flooding.  The Little Kanawha River created around 28.3 feet in Glenville.  Flood stage is 23 feet.  Four homes had water inside in Sand Fork.  The Red Cross opened a shelter in Sand Fork with at least 4 families being housed.  One home had major damage in the county, while 10 others had minor damage.  One business had 4 inches of water on its first floor. In the city of Glenville, a few homes had water in their basements.  The emergency manager said, We've been very lucky with only a few homes and buinesses getting water inside.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 34 year old woman was killed, when a 70 foot tall tree fell up a slight hill and smashed through the middle of her mobile home.  She was sitting inside watching television.  The freak accident occurred as the heavier rains were diminishing.  The ground was saturated with some soil erosion around the tree. The wind was blowing at about 10 to 20 mph.  Her husband and 2 children were also inside.  Her 6 year old daughter was slightly injured with abrasions on her leg.||Emergency responders were slowed by water over the roads from small streams.  A fire truck became stuck in the water and had to be removed by a large tow truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Instability was less than the 2nd of June, but thunderstorms still formed south of a cold front that was sinking through West Virginia.  Some gusty winds occurred, but were below severe limits. There was one report of marginally severe hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Little Kanawha River and its feeder streams flooded roads and homes.  Later that day, the main stem of the Little Kanawha River flooded.  The crest at Grantsville was just over 1 foot about flood stage, cresting near 37 feet overnight on May 4th into the 5th. In the county, 2 homes had major damage, while several other homes received minor damage. Three businesses also received damage. The elememtary school in Arnoldsburg  got about 17 to 18 inches of water inside.  School employees moved vulnerable supplies to higher ground before the water spilled inside. One church had a basement classroom damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex moved from eastern Kentucky into West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 8th.  In the wake of that feature, a strong upper air disturbance helped refire convection over southern Kentucky.  Those thunderstorms moved east into southern West Virginia just after dark.  One of these cells had strong rotation in McDowell County. No tornadoes were observed, but wind damage was reported.     ||The storms lost their rotation later that evening, but a narrow broken train of showers and thunderstorms continued to roll east out of Kentucky and into southern West Virginia after midnight, during the early morning hours of Saturday, May 9th.  This west to east train of showers and thunderstorms affected mainly eastern Mingo, Wyoming, northern McDowell, and extreme southern Raleigh Counties.  The heaviest rains were mostly from 2300E on the 8th to 0400E on the 9th. The 12 hour rain amounts from 1700E on the 8th through 0500E on the 9th were mostly 2.5 to 4 inches in the west to east band.  The north to south width of this heavy rain axis was only about 30 miles. A few rain amounts exceeded 4 inches, mainly near the county border with Mingo and Wyoming.  Amounts may have exceeded 4 inches over the mountains south of Pineville.  The highest measured 24 hour total was 4.4 inches from R.D. Bailey Lake.  An automatic gauge on Horsepen Mountain measured 3.98 inches.  In Wyoming County, Pineville had 3.95 inches, while Mullens measured 3.02 inches.  Oceana was just north of the heaviest, with 2.76 inches.  Meanwhile, Iaeger of McDowell County was just south of the heaviest axis.  Iaeger measured 2.75 inches.  An automatic gauge along Huff Knob in southern Raleigh County measured 2.66 inches.   ||Small stream flooding increased after 0130E on the 9th.  The peak of the small stream flooding was on either side of dawn.  Water cascaded down steep mountainsides, sometimes creating their own water channels and landslides.  Rocks were swept down these mountainsides, collecting 3 to 4 feet deep in the valleys.  Trees were also knocked down by these landslides, some falling into homes.   ||The worst of the small stream flooding occurred along such creeks at Pigeon, Horesepen, Gilbert, Bens, Beech, and Mate Creeks in eastern Mingo County, plus Huff, Indian, and Pinnacle Creeks in Wyoming County.  This included such communities as Pie, Musick, Varney, Taylorsville, Delbarton, Red Jacket, North Matewan, Gilbert, Baisden, Hanover, and Fanrock.  However, the serious flooding still stretched further east, to encompass the headwaters of the Guyandotte River, upstream of Mullens.  This included the Rhodell vicinity in southern Raleigh County.||Many residents along these small streams were forced to seek higher ground and wait out the flood.  Luckily, no one drowned.||Some of the heavier rain actually fell east of Guyandotte Mountain and flooded the headwaters of Piney Creek.  Piney Creek flows north into the Kanawha River.  This includes the Jonben vicinity of Raleigh County. ||The small stream flooding lead to river flooding along the Guyandotte River. Man crested near 17.8 feet near dawn on the 9th.  Flood stage is 15 feet. Further down the river, Logan crested around its 23 foot flood stage during the mid morning hours.  R. D. Bailey Dam held back much of the headwater flow.  The lake elevation rose about 65 feet in the vertical over 4 days, from around 1045 feet to a crest of 1109.85 feet on May 12th.  That was the second highest lake pool elevation for that U.S. Army Corps of Engineers facility. ||The Tug Fork River also flooded, but the most serious damage in that river drainage system was still related to the small stream flooding and the runoff down the steep mountainsides.  Williamson crested at 40.7 feet near sunset that Saturday evening.  Flood stage is 27 feet but a flood wall protects the city.  Later that night, Kermit crested at 43.2 feet.  Flood stage is 38 feet at Kermit.||Six miners and 1 supervisor were temporarily trapped in an underground mine near Wharncliffe due to high water.  These miners finally walked out during the early morning of Sunday the 10th. ||President Obama issued a major disaster declaration for Mingo, Wyoming, Raleigh, and McDowell Counties.  Over 2300 people from Mingo County registered with FEMA for assistance.  Almost 1200 Wyoming County residents registered.  McDowell County had around 500 register with FEMA, while Raleigh County saw under 300.  A good indication of the number of homes destroyed is the number of households provided with temporary housing assistance.  Over 100 home owners asked for temporary housing.  Around two-thirds of those came from Mingo County, with the other third from Wyoming County.  See FEMA disaster number 1838 for more details.||State preliminary damage assessments had over 300 other homes with major damage.  Several hundred houses had minor damage.  Numerous vehicles were flooded, some even swept into the creeks.  Many small private bridges for residents were destroyed. The main state highway system was also damaged. In Mingo County 5 primary roads and 46 secondary roads were damaged.  In Wyoming County, 4 primary roads and 76 secondary roads were damaged. In McDowell County 4 primary and 34 secondary roads were damaged. Raleigh County had 9 secondary roads damaged. Several schools were flooded.  Gilbert Elementary and Gilbert High were hard hit.  Many small public water systems were damaged.||At one point, the number of Army and Air National Guard troops, assisting in the clean up, reached over 600. The Commonwealth of Virginia sent around 30 National Guard troops to assist.  The National Guard removed around 58,000 tons of debris in Mingo County and about 7,000 tons from Wyoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down.

A front was stretched out east to west near the southern border of West Virginia. A strong mid level disturbance lifted northeast and enhanced the rain after 0100E on Monday the 4th before lifting out around  noon.  The axis of the heaviest rain fell from southern Wayne County on northeast, through western Kanawaha County, southern Roane County, southern Calhoun County, then through Gilmer, Lewis, and Harrison Counties.  The rain totals in this maximum were mostly 2 to 2.9 inches in a 6 to 12 hour period.||Leading up to this episode, the ground was saturated from light but frequent rain. The spring foliage was just in its beginning stages, and not consuming a lot of soil moisture.  As a result, the rain quickly became runoff.||Nuiisance flooding was common, with water ponding on roads in poor drainage areas.  More serious stream flooding occurred along the West Fork of the Little Kanawha River in Calhoun County. Schools were canceled in a few counties. ||River flooding resulted along the Little Kanawha River downstream of Burnsville through Glenville to Grantsville.  The West Fork River also flooded as it flowed north through Harrison County.||In June, some of these counties were added to FEMA disaster 1838.  Around 3 dozen home owners applied for assistance.
