Two slow moving clusters of showers and thunderstorms moved through the St. Albans vicinity during the late afternoon of this Memorial Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in St. Albans measured 3.85 inches for its 24 hours total, but most of it fell in less than 2 hours.  Around 2 inches fell in less than 30 minutes.||The ground was already wetter than normal prior to the event. So the runoff quickly flowed off hillsides and through the streets.  The urban drainage system was overwhelmed.  Low spots quickly filled up with water.  About 50 to 60 basements were flooded.  The city pool saw water and mud flow into it.  These localized downpours reached across the Kanawha river and flooded a few homes in Nitro.  A few vehicles stalled in the water and their drivers had to be helped out.||The city of St. Albans ended up with a monthly rain total of 12.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms approached western West Virginia from southern Ohio and northern Kentucky by early evening on the 30th. The region was in a northwest flow aloft.  Some of the thunderstorms strengthened, with high reflectivity cores observed on weather radar. This resulted in some large hail reaching the ground.  Damaging wind gusts also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of showers and thunderstorms formed in Ohio and West Virginia. Surface dew points were in the low and mid 70s.  The less organized clusters, that were in West Virginia, began to train along a portion of the Interstate 64 corridor during the evening.  This area was already wet from previous rains.  The downpours tended to build west from Putnam County back into Cabell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Hurricane vicinity, 3 inches of rain was measured in 2 hours between 1600 to 1800E, with another inch in the hour between 1900 to 2000E.  Another nearby measurement had a 3 inch rain total.  A third report from a spotter, indicated 3.5 inches of rain, with an inch falling in 30 minutes. ||Small stream and urban flooding were common.  Scary Creek flooded roads and surrounded a few homes.  The Hurricane reservoir overflowed its banks, closing Teays Valley Road.  A small impoundment overflowed near Hurricane, resulting in water flowing through a subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of showers and thunderstorms formed in Ohio and West Virginia. Surface dew points were in the low and mid 70s.  The less organized clusters, that were in West Virginia, began to train along a portion of the Interstate 64 corridor during the evening.  This area was already wet from previous rains.  The downpours tended to build west from Putnam County back into Cabell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of 3 to 3.5 inches fell in about 3 hours.  Pea Ridge measured 3.3 inches. Small streams, such as Cyrus Creek, Fudges Creek, and Charley Creek flooded roads. Water got into a few homes.  A truck got into high water along Merrick Creek, just north of Barboursville.  There was also urban flooding near the Barboursville Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced very heavy rain on the rate of 3 to 4 inches an hour at times. Rainfall from this thunderstorm caused a culvert pipe to wash out on Wilderness Road due to drainage ditch flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a culvert pipe to be washed out on Wilderness Road from drainage ditch flooding. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized downpours occurred during the predawn hours in Wayne and Boone Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent showers and thunderstorms dumped 1.5 to 2 inches of rain, mostly in 3 to 6 hours during the late night and predawn.  Madison reported a total of 1.9 inches.  Water briefly blocked some roads, such as along Camp Creek and Rock Creek.  A lady stalled her van in water along Route 3 and Rock Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the muggy summer air, ahead of a cold front in western Ohio, surface dew points were in the upper 60s.   A few narrow bands of showers and thunderstorms formed overnight.  The rain was oriented from southwest to northeast, parallel to their movement.  Early on the 29th, the furthest east band continued to form new cells over Kanawha County.  As a result, repetitive cells moved along the same corridor in  Clay County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2100E on the 28th through 0300E on the 29th, a narrow southwest to northeast band of 3 to 5 inches of rain fell, mainly near and just east of the Elk River.   The Department of Highways garage in Clay measured 4.1 inches.  The weekend rain total from the Clay cooperative observer was 5.25 inches, with the vast majority of that rain falling during this event.   ||Streams such as Beechy Creek, Middle Creek, and Buffalo Creek flooded.  This included areas around Hartland, Clay, and Dundon.  However, most of the damage was from small debris flows off the hillsides.  These small debris flows brought rocks and mud into yards and flooded basements.  ||The most significant damage occurred at 2 residences on Hartland Road.  Debris flowed into Beechy Creek and blocked a culvert.  This caused the creek to create a temporary lake behind the culvert.  Since the residences were surrounded by hillsides, the water got over 10 feet deep.  The water level reached over the windows on the one home closest to the culvert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of showers and thunderstorms formed in Ohio and West Virginia. Surface dew points were in the low and mid 70s.  The less organized clusters, that were in West Virginia, began to train along a portion of the Interstate 64 corridor during the evening.  This area was already wet from previous rains.  The downpours tended to build west from Putnam County back into Cabell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Mason County 911 center.  An employee standing outside, was unsure whether the lightning hit the tower, the metal roof, or the lightning rods on top of the roof. ||The lightning knocked out the center's radio and telephone communiciations, as well as its computer and security system.  The center worked out of a mobile unit for about a week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the muggy air, out ahead of a cold front, downpours occurred during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Armstrong Creek flooded around Kimberly.  Roads and bridges were damaged.  The water system to 250 families was also damaged.||In the vicinity, Marmet Lock reported 2.5 inches of rain in 24 hours, but London Lock had 1.8 inches.  Most of the rain fell in a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the muggy air, out ahead of a cold front, downpours occurred during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lower portions of Cabin Creek flooded.  Water backed up behind the bridges near Sharon and Rhonda compounding the flooding.   One house had 18 inches of water inside.  Water was also in a church basement. ||In the vicinity, Marmet Lock reported 2.5 inches of rain in 24 hours, but London Lock had 1.8 inches.  Most of the rain fell in a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the muggy air, out ahead of a cold front, downpours occurred during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two Lick Run flooded roads.  Rain of 1.5 inches likely fell in less than 2 hours on relatively wet ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the muggy air, out ahead of a cold front, downpours occurred during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small streams and runs flooded roads.  Rain amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches likely fell in a few hours, after over an inch fell the previous day.  There was minor damage to the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized downpours occurred during the predawn hours in Wayne and Boone Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rains of 1.5 to 2 inches, falling mostly between 0300 and 0500E, caused Mill Creek to flood.  Water got into a few vulnerable homes, including a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolong steady rain resulted in rain amounts on either side of 2 inches. No significant flooding occurred due to the previous dry conditions and the lack of excessive rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite no significant flooding, Indian Creek and Flatwood Run near Pinch were around bankfull during the afternoon.  This helped water to pond in a poor drainage area around the intersection of the Pinch and Quick Roads.  This included the parking lot of a convenience store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolong steady rain resulted in rain amounts on either side of 2 inches. No significant flooding occurred due to the previous dry conditions and the lack of excessive rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The viaducts in the city of Huntington were closed to vehicular traffic during the late morning into the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe late afternoon thunderstorms on June 2nd developed in a warm, unstable air mass ahead of a slow-moving frontal boundary located north of the area. Numerous penny to quarter-size hail reports were received.

Severe late afternoon thunderstorms on June 2nd developed in a warm, unstable air mass ahead of a slow-moving frontal boundary located north of the area. Numerous penny to quarter-size hail reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds brought down several large trees on Moore Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable air mass and seasonably strong mid-level shear helped to produce multicellular storms which produced widely scattered areas of damaging winds during the mid-morning hours of the June 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeast low level flow existed into the mountainous counties as an upper level short wave passed. The freezing level was around 12 thousand feet. A few storms pulsed to marginally severe limits and dumped some large hail.

A strong low level wind jet, in advance of an approaching cold front, combined with daytime heating to produce strong to severe thunderstorms.  The storms produced some severe wind gusts and large hail.

Showers and thunderstorms dumped 1.5 to 2 inches of rain between 0000E and 0800E, with upwards of an inch during one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street and poor drainage flooding was common.  The railroad viaducts were closed within the city of Huntington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure storm system was lifting northeast through central Kentucky during the late afternoon, then through Ohio overnight.   Southeast winds of 40 to 50 mph were felt over the higher terrain during the day.  A line of showers swept rapidly through West Virginia during the late evening of the 2nd into the early morning hours of the 3rd.  Gusts of 40 to 50 mph from the southwest were felt in a few areas with that line of showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two empty box trailers being towed across the New River Gorge Bridge on Route 19 flipped over in the wind.  The road was closed for a brief time.  ||Across the whole county, about 2500 customers lost electricity during this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A canopy over a gas station in Grafton was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms formed just in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of these storms strengthened to severe levels and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were brought down. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms dropped southeast out of southern Ohio and dumped heavy rain across northern Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In one hour, 2.21 inches of rain was measured at the Tri State Airport.  Rain totals peaked around 2.4 inches. Street and poor drainage flooding was common across northern Wayne County, including the western limits of Huntington.  Storm drains were inundated.  Vehicles stalled out in a few spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually deep mid level trough for mid July was in place over the Ohio Valley.  The resulting cool pool of air aloft helped to generate instability showers and thunderstorms.  Despite marginal moisture, a few spots in Nicholas and Fayette Counties received 2 to 3 inches of rain.  This was due to the slow movement of the cells and some back building characteristics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams overflowed and damaged culverts in the Upper Glade Creek vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass ahead of a cold front helped with the formation of thunderstorms. Some of the storms had torrential rains which caused flash flooding over portions of Southeast West Virginia during the evening of August 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brush Creek was out of its banks, and flooding three basements in the Green Valley area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grassy Branch came out of its banks, and flooded a couple houses and garages on Grassy Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the Ohio Valley triggered thunderstorms ahead of it during the afternoon of August 11th. One thunderstorm produced damaging winds just north of Lewisburg, WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were uprooted and several were snapped off above the ground, along Interstate 64, between Fairview Road and Circle Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe late afternoon thunderstorms on June 2nd developed in a warm, unstable air mass ahead of a slow-moving frontal boundary located north of the area. Numerous penny to quarter-size hail reports were received.
In the muggy air, out ahead of a cold front, downpours occurred during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of Twelvepole Creek flooded portions of Dunlow and Route 152.  An elderly woman in a stalled vehicle was rescued by the fire department.  Dunlow measured 2.2 inches of rain in their 24 hour report.  Most of that rain fell in a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced scattered severe thunderstorms across southeast Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and power lines down in Morgantown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a front staying just to the north and west, thunderstorms developed around midday with daytime heating.  Due to drier air in the mid levels, the thunderstorms were not as widespread as compared to the coverage on the 8th.  However, a few storms still produced large hail.

A line of strong thunderstorms moved into the area in association with an approaching cold front. Some of the storms along the line increased to severe levels, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew trees down. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms approached western West Virginia from southern Ohio and northern Kentucky by early evening on the 30th. The region was in a northwest flow aloft.  Some of the thunderstorms strengthened, with high reflectivity cores observed on weather radar. This resulted in some large hail reaching the ground.  Damaging wind gusts also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Route 52 from Delbarton to the Varney vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms approached western West Virginia from southern Ohio and northern Kentucky by early evening on the 30th. The region was in a northwest flow aloft.  Some of the thunderstorms strengthened, with high reflectivity cores observed on weather radar. This resulted in some large hail reaching the ground.  Damaging wind gusts also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak upper air disturbances helped trigger afternoon convection in a very moist environment, ahead of an approaching cold front.  The front was in northwest Ohio, Indiana and western Kentucky.  Clusters and short lines of thunderstorms formed in the late afternoon and again overnight.  The steering flow had increased to around 25 knots.  Storms pulsed briefly to severe limits with reports of both strong winds and large hail.

A potent cold front passed through eastern West Virginia on the evening of the 11th. A warm moist flow ahead of the front caused more instability than usual for this time of year. The combination of the cold front and instability caused showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some thunderstorms were severe, producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted along Morgan Grove Road near Shepherdstown.
